claim: The first critical rule when a dog gets loose is to NOT call or chase the dog — instead sit down, look away, and offer treats; chasing triggers flight instinct.
stance_target: no-chase protocol as immediate first-response rule for any escaped dog
scope_conditions: any dog that has not immediately returned; especially socialized dogs that may still be caught in the immediate window
quoted_support: If your dog gets loose and does not come back to you quickly, DO NOT CALL OR CHASE THE DOG! Try sitting down and looking away, and offering treats if you have any with you.

claim: Flyers should be posted within a 1-mile radius of the last-seen point immediately — on street signs, in apartment complexes, distributed to all neighbors, and shared on local social media (lost dog Facebook pages, NextDoor, Ring).
stance_target: immediate 1-mile radius flyer campaign as early-phase priority action
scope_conditions: first 24 hours; 1-mile radius is the minimum — sightings may require expansion
quoted_support: Make a flyer immediately and put up flyers in a one-mile radius around where the pet was lost.

claim: Feeding stations should use highly aromatic food (rotisserie chicken deboned, tuna, sardines, canned cat food, kielbasa, hot dogs) monitored by camera — camera confirmation of who ate the food is emphasized as best practice.
stance_target: aromatic food types and camera monitoring as feeding station protocol
scope_conditions: near last-seen point; cellular trail cameras are preferred for remote monitoring
quoted_support: Set up one or more feeding stations close to where pet was lost. Use food with a strong smell, like rotisserie chicken (de-boned), Royal Farms chicken, tuna fish, sardines, canned cat food, kielbasa, hotdogs. It's best to monitor the food with a camera so you know if the dog or another animal ate the food.
